title: Fast Like a Girl vs The Obesity Code: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Compare Fast Like a Girl vs The Obesity Code. Detailed head-to-head comparison with strengths, weaknesses, and expert verdict. Which book is right for you?

book1.title: Fast Like a Girl
book1.subtitle: A Woman's Guide to Using the Healing Power of Fasting to Burn Fat, Boost Energy, and Balance Hormones
book1.author: Dr. Mindy Pelz
book1.description: Renowned women's health expert Dr. Mindy Pelz has created an accessible, all-in-one guide to intermittent fasting specifically designed for women. In Fast Like a Girl, she reveals how women can fast in a way that supports their unique hormonal needs throughout the different phases of their menstrual cycle, from their fertile years through perimenopause and menopause.

book2.title: The Obesity Code
book2.subtitle: Unlocking the Secrets of Weight Loss
book2.author: Dr. Jason Fung
book2.description: Everything you believe about how to lose weight is wrong. Weight gain and obesity are driven by hormonesâ€”in everyoneâ€”and only by understanding the effects of insulin and insulin resistance can we achieve lasting weight loss. Dr. Jason Fung reveals why interventions such as weight-loss surgery can be lifesaving and why we must examine our underlying hormonal imbalances. The Obesity Code is revolutionary and has the power to change the conversation around weight loss and health.

table.book1: More complex - varies by cycle phase

table.book2: Established research on insulin and fasting

book1Strengths: The cycle-syncing fasting protocol is revolutionary: fast 13-15 hours during follicular phase (days 1-10), push to 15-17 hours during ovulation (days 11-15), ease back to 13-15 during luteal phase (days 16-19), then NO fasting or only light 12-hour fasts during menstruation (days 1-5). This respects that women's cortisol, estrogen, and progesterone need different fuel timing

Dr. Mindy Pelz's credentials are solid—she's been researching women's fasting for years, has a huge YouTube following (2M+ subscribers), and actually practices on herself and patients. Not just theory—real clinical experience with thousands of women

The hormone hierarchy explanation is brilliant: if cortisol is dysregulated (from too much fasting), your sex hormones (estrogen, progesterone, testosterone) can't function. Women who fast like men often crash their hormones. This explains why 16:8 daily fasting wrecks some women

Covers ALL life stages—fertile years (cycle-sync your fasting), perimenopause (longer fasts work better as estrogen drops), menopause (you can fast more aggressively). Finally a book that doesn't assume all women are 25 and cycling regularly
42,000 ratings at 4.6 stars—published 2022, already massive following. Women are DESPERATE for this information because standard fasting advice has been failing them

The autophagy explanation for women is phenomenal: 17+ hour fasts trigger cellular cleanup, but women need to time this with their cycle. Do it wrong (fasting too much during luteal/menstrual), and you'll be exhausted and moody. Do it right, and you'll feel superhuman
book1Weaknesses: The cycle-tracking requirement is real work—you need to know where you are in your cycle, which means tracking ovulation, periods, and symptoms. If you have irregular cycles or PCOS, this gets complicated fast. Not beginner-friendly

The science on gender-specific fasting is still emerging—most fasting studies are done on men or male rats. Dr. Pelz extrapolates from limited female-specific research. It makes biological sense, but we need more RCTs on women before declaring this definitive

If you're post-menopausal or on hormonal birth control (which suppresses your natural cycle), the cycle-syncing protocol doesn't apply. You'll need to adapt, and the book gives some guidance but it's not as detailed

The book can feel overwhelming—six different fasting protocols (Intermittent, Autophagy, Gut Reset, Fat Burner, Dopamine Reset, Immune Reset) PLUS cycle-syncing PLUS supplement recommendations. Where do you even start?

Some critics (including doctors) question whether this complexity is necessary or if it's just marketing. The 'women aren't small men' framing is valid, but does fasting timing really need to be THIS precise? Jury's still out
Not for men—if you bought this as a couple, half of you can't use the main protocol. The Obesity Code works for everyone
book2Strengths: The insulin theory of obesity is revolutionary: it's not calories in/calories out, it's about lowering insulin to unlock fat burning. Dr. Fung destroys the 'eat less, move more' myth with actual endocrinology. Insulin blocks fat burning—keep it elevated with constant eating, and you can't lose weight no matter the calorie deficit

Dr. Jason Fung is a nephrologist (kidney doctor) who treats diabetic patients with fasting instead of drugs. He's reversed Type 2 diabetes in thousands of patients through his Intensive Dietary Management program. This isn't theory—he's done it clinically for years

The fasting protocols are dead simple: 16:8 (skip breakfast), 20:4 (one meal daily), 24-hour fasts (dinner to dinner), 36-hour fasts (occasional). Pick one, stick with it. No cycle-tracking, no complexity. Works for men and women equally

67,000 ratings at 4.6 stars—published 2016, proven track record. This is THE fasting book that launched the intermittent fasting movement into mainstream. Bigger audience than Fast Like a Girl because it works for everyone
The research is rock-solid—Fung cites decades of metabolic studies on insulin, glucagon, autophagy, and fasting. This isn't emerging science, it's established endocrinology applied to weight loss

Works for weight loss AND reversing metabolic disease—insulin resistance, Type 2 diabetes, fatty liver. Fung shows fasting isn't just for losing pounds, it's for healing metabolic dysfunction at the root
book2Weaknesses: Zero acknowledgment that women's hormones complicate fasting—no mention of menstrual cycles, fertility concerns, or why some women feel terrible on rigid 16:8 protocols. Fung's approach is 'just fast,' which works great for men and some women, but crashes others
Published 2016—predates the explosion of research on women-specific fasting. The science has evolved, and we now know women need more nuance. Fung's book doesn't reflect this
The one-size-fits-all approach can harm women with hormone issues—PCOS, hypothyroid, adrenal fatigue, perimenopause. These women often need gentler, cycle-aware fasting. Fung doesn't differentiate

Some women report losing their periods, crashing energy, or worsening thyroid function on Fung's protocols. He doesn't address this risk. Fast Like a Girl explains WHY this happens and how to prevent it
Doesn't cover perimenopause or menopause—massive oversight given that fasting affects women differently at different life stages. Fast Like a Girl dedicates entire chapters to this
The male default problem—most fasting studies cited are on men. Fung extrapolates to women without acknowledging the gap in research

book1: Women are not small men. We need to fast differently.
Your menstrual cycle is not your enemyâ€”it's your superpower.
Fasting is a tool, and like any tool, it needs to be used correctly.
The key to fasting success for women is hormone variability.
You can't hack what you don't track.
When you fast in alignment with your cycle, magic happens.
book2: Obesity is a hormonal, not a caloric, imbalance.
Insulin is the main driver of weight gain. Reduce insulin, lose weight.
Intermittent fasting is the most efficient way to lower insulin levels.
The calories-in, calories-out model has failed us.
Type 2 diabetes is reversible through dietary changes.
We must address the hormonal imbalance, not just count calories.

book1: You're a woman who has struggled with traditional fasting
You want to understand how fasting affects female hormones
You're willing to track your menstrual cycle for better results
You're in perimenopause or menopause and need specific guidance
You want an approach that respects women's unique biology
You've had negative experiences with one-size-fits-all fasting
You're interested in optimizing hormonal health through fasting
book2: You want a simple, straightforward fasting protocol
You're focused on weight loss and reversing insulin resistance
You prefer established research over emerging theories
You're a man or prefer gender-neutral approaches
You want comprehensive understanding of why we gain weight
You don't want to track menstrual cycles or complicate things
You're ready for a proven, time-tested approach

verdict.summary: Fast Like a Girl wins for women with 42,000 ratings at 4.6 stars—cycle-syncing fasting protocol (13-15 hours follicular, 15-17 ovulation, ease back luteal, minimal during menstruation) respects female hormone fluctuations. The Obesity Code wins for simplicity and bigger proof with 67,000 at 4.6 stars—insulin theory, simple 16:8 protocol, works for everyone. Different needs: women wanting hormone-aware fasting choose Fast Like a Girl, people wanting proven simplicity choose Obesity Code. Men and post-menopausal women default to Fung.
verdict.recommendation: If you're a cycling woman (pre-menopause, not on hormonal birth control), read Fast Like a Girl first. The cycle-syncing protocol (13-15 hour fasts during follicular, 15-17 during ovulation, ease back during luteal, minimal during menstruation), hormone hierarchy explanation (cortisol dysregulation crashes sex hormones), and six fasting protocols (Intermittent, Autophagy, Gut Reset, Fat Burner, Dopamine Reset, Immune Reset) will prevent the hormone crashes some women get from rigid daily fasting. THEN read The Obesity Code for the foundational insulin science that applies to everyone. If you're a man, post-menopausal, or want simplicity over precision, start with The Obesity Code. Fung's insulin theory (lower insulin to unlock fat burning), simple protocols (16:8, 20:4, 24-hour fasts), and proven clinical results (reversing Type 2 diabetes in thousands) are straightforward and effective. The ideal: cycling women read both (Fast Like a Girl for protocol, Obesity Code for science), everyone else reads Obesity Code first.
